title: Namma Cloud
text:
A Pitch for India's Banking Future


1 Namma Cloud
India's sovereign, full-stack cloud infrastructure platform — built exclusively for banks, compliant by design, and powered by the team that built India's payments.


$1 Tn+Annualised TPV


300 Mn+Transactions/Day


99.999%Uptime Target


10,000+Peak TPS


0%Technical Declines







Foreword


2 Why This Book Exists
The infrastructure that powers Indian banking was never designed for what banking has become. This book is about fixing that — permanently.
In 2012, Juspay built the SDK that let Indians auto-read OTPs on mobile. In 2016, we designed the BHIM app and the core UPI library. Over the next decade, we touched nearly every layer of India's digital payments stack — not as observers, but as builders.
That journey taught us something uncomfortable: the platforms we build are only as good as the infrastructure they run on. And today, that infrastructure — for most Indian banks — is a patchwork of ageing on-premise hardware, dollar-denominated hyperscaler contracts, and manual operations held together by institutional knowledge and hope.

"The platforms we build are only as good as the infrastructure they run on. And India's banking infrastructure needs to be rebuilt — from the ground up, for India."
— Juspay Infrastructure Team

Namma Cloud is our answer. It is a sovereign, full-stack cloud infrastructure platform — built in India, for Indian banks, on Indian soil. It offers everything AWS, GCP, and Azure offer, but without the FX exposure, without the compliance risk, and without the vendor lock-in that has trapped every bank in the country.
This book tells that story — the problem, the solution, the capabilities, and the path forward.







📖 How to Read This Book

This book is structured in four parts. Part I makes the case for why the status quo is no longer acceptable. Part II introduces Namma Cloud and the team behind it. Part III is a deep technical and commercial dive. Part IV outlines the migration journey and long-term vision. CXOs may wish to read Parts I, II, and IV first.

title: 2  The Sovereign Imperative
text:
Chapter 1
The geopolitical moment has arrived. Sovereign cloud is no longer a philosophical preference — it is a regulatory requirement, a national security posture, and an economic necessity.

title: 2  The Sovereign Imperative
text:
2.1 The World Has Changed
For most of the 2010s, "cloud" meant AWS or Azure. Cheap, scalable, globally available. The tradeoffs — data crossing borders, dollar-denominated costs, dependence on foreign infrastructure — felt abstract. They no longer do.
The Russia-Ukraine war demonstrated that global payment rails can be severed overnight. US-China tariff escalations showed that technology supply chains are weaponisable. India's own experience with data localisation debates, the Aadhaar controversy, and NPCI's insistence on data sovereignty have all pointed in one direction: critical national infrastructure must be controlled nationally.



🌍


2.1.1 Geopolitical Volatility
Russia-Ukraine, Iran tensions, and US-China decoupling have made dependency on foreign cloud a sovereign risk — not just a technical one.




⚖️


2.1.2 Regulatory Pressure
RBI, NPCI, and MeitY are each moving — independently and simultaneously — toward mandating data residency and sovereign hosting for critical financial data.




💱


2.1.3 FX Economics
Dollar-denominated cloud bills have not followed Moore's Law. Banks earning in INR face 15–30% cost inflation purely from currency exposure.

title: 2  The Sovereign Imperative
text:
2.2 MeitY's March 2026 Directive
In a March 2026 office memorandum titled Guidelines for Cloud Selection Framework, the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology directed all ministries and departments to classify their data into three categories before selecting any cloud service:







🔴 Top Secret / Secret

Strictly not permitted to be hosted on commercial cloud platforms at all. Must remain on sovereign, air-gapped infrastructure.










🟡 Category A — Sensitive National Data

Includes Aadhaar, PAN, e-Courts, UPI data, passport records, railways, tax systems, voter ID (central); land records, revenue courts, treasury (state). Must be hosted on government cloud services or sovereign-certified providers. No commercial hyperscalers.










🟢 Category B — Official Use Data

RTI-disclosable data, welfare scheme data, public grievances. May be hosted on central/state cloud services or MeitY-notified sovereign providers.



The implication for banks is unambiguous: UPI transaction data, card data, Aadhaar-linked KYC — everything that makes modern banking work — cannot legally sit on AWS, GCP, or Azure. The only compliant path is a sovereign cloud certified by MeitY, or an on-premise infrastructure operated in India.

title: 2  The Sovereign Imperative
text:
2.3 India's RBI Position
The Reserve Bank of India has long maintained that payment system data must be stored exclusively in India. The 2018 circular on Payment System Data Storage made this explicit for UPI and card networks. The 2023 Master Directions on Outsourcing extended this to cloud service providers, requiring banks to ensure data sovereignty, right to audit, and exit clauses.







💡 The Compliance Trap

Banks are caught in a bind: modern cloud-native architecture requires hyperscalers, but RBI mandates on-premise or sovereign hosting. Legacy on-prem is too brittle for modern workloads. The only escape is a sovereign cloud that is actually cloud-native — which is exactly what Namma Cloud is.

title: 2  The Sovereign Imperative
text:
2.4 The Window Is Narrow
Banks that move to sovereign cloud in the next 18–24 months will build on modern, AI-powered infrastructure that gives them a 10-year cost and capability advantage. Banks that wait will face forced migrations under regulatory pressure, with less choice, less time, and higher risk.
The sovereign moment is here. The question is not whether to move, but with whom.

title: 3  The Bank's Infrastructure Crisis
text:
Chapter 2
Most banks in India are running critical workloads on infrastructure that was never designed for the transaction volumes, regulatory requirements, or operational models of 2026. This is not a technical problem. It is an existential one.

title: 3  The Bank's Infrastructure Crisis
text:
3.1 Six Interlocking Failures
The infrastructure crisis facing Indian banks is not one problem — it is six problems that reinforce each other. Fixing one in isolation does not help. The only solution is a complete rethink.




The Compliance Trap

Banks are caught between RBI's on-premise data localisation mandate and the operational reality that legacy on-prem systems cannot handle modern UPI volumes, latency requirements, or availability targets. Public cloud is non-compliant. Legacy on-prem is non-performant. There is no middle ground — until now.





Infrastructure Debt

Decades of patchwork on-premise infrastructure — heterogeneous hardware, undocumented configurations, single points of failure, manual provisioning — means that the average Indian bank cannot scale its infrastructure in under a week. During salary season, festival periods, or tax filing deadlines, this becomes a crisis every single year.





The Cost Spiral

Dollar-denominated AWS and GCP bills consume an increasingly large share of IT budgets. Cloud costs have not followed Moore's Law — in fact, as workloads grow and FX moves against the rupee, per-transaction infrastructure costs are rising. Meanwhile, wastage rates of 30–40% are common because capacity planning is done manually and conservatively.





The Operations Burden

Hundreds of engineers in every large bank spend their days managing infrastructure instead of building banking products. There is no single pane of glass. A typical bank runs 10+ tools to operate a single Kubernetes cluster. Deployments are weekly batch events, not continuous. Every release carries risk.





Zero Observability

Most banks find out about infrastructure failures from customer complaints, not from their own monitoring. There is no unified alert system, no predictive anomaly detection, no capacity forecasting. Every salary day, every GST filing deadline, every IPO subscription window is a gamble.





Vendor Lock-in

Proprietary managed services from hyperscalers — RDS, DynamoDB, CloudWatch, Lambda — make migration prohibitively expensive. Every bank that chose the "easy" path of managed services 5 years ago is now paying the hyperscaler tax with no exit. The managed services they depend on are Category-A data violations waiting to happen.




"Every one of these six failures has a single root cause: there is no trusted, sovereign, full-stack cloud built for Indian banking. Until now."

title: 4  Why Juspay
text:
Chapter 3
Building a cloud for banks requires two things most infrastructure companies don't have: deep banking domain knowledge and battle-tested reliability at national scale. Juspay has both.

title: 4  Why Juspay
text:

Juspay did not enter the payments space as a gateway or aggregator. We entered as infrastructure builders. Our work sits beneath the apps, beneath the banks, at the layer where reliability is not a feature — it is the product.



2012



India's First OTP Auto-Read SDK

Juspay Safe SDK — the first SDK to auto-read OTPs for mobile payments, reducing friction for 300 million mobile banking users.




2016



Built BHIM and the Core UPI Library

Juspay does not operate at startup scale. We operate at national infrastructure scale. The demands of processing $1 trillion in annualised transaction value — reliably, at millisecond latency, with zero technical declines — forced us to build infrastructure capability that no third-party cloud could deliver.


$1 Tn+ Annualised Transaction Value


300 Mn+ Transactions Processed Daily

500+ Enterprise Banks & Merchants

title: 4  Why Juspay
section: 4.3 We Know What You Don't Know You Need
text:
4.3 We Know What You Don't Know You Need
A traditional cloud provider builds infrastructure for generic workloads. Juspay builds infrastructure that has processed every edge case in Indian banking — salary runs on the 1st of every month, GST filing surges, IPO subscription windows, RBI system maintenance windows, NPCI outages, and more.
Our infrastructure was not designed in a lab. It was designed under fire — 300 million transactions a day, with a bank's reputation on the line for each one.







✅ The Juspay Advantage

We are the only cloud provider that has operated at the intersection of Indian banking, NPCI infrastructure, and cloud-native engineering at national scale. Namma Cloud is not a product we built for banks — it is the infrastructure we built to survive building for banks.

title: 5  Introducing Namma Cloud
text:
Chapter 4
Namma Cloud is a fully sovereign, AI-powered, end-to-end cloud infrastructure platform built exclusively for Indian banks. It is on par with — and in critical ways better than — AWS, GCP, and Azure.

title: 5  Introducing Namma Cloud
text:
5.1 One Platform. The Entire Stack.
Most cloud infrastructure offerings solve one layer. A hardware colocation. A managed Kubernetes service. A monitoring tool. Namma Cloud solves the entire stack — from physical servers to running applications — and every layer is designed to work with every other layer.



Layer 4



Juspay Banking Products


UPI Stack · Cards PG · Orchestrator · FASTag · IBMB · JusTrust · BBPS





Layer 3



Namma Cloud Platform


InfraSwitch · Autopilot · Styrofoam · Namma Observe · Namma DB





Layer 2



Compute, Storage & Orchestration


OpenStack · Ceph · Kubernetes · Containers · Object Storage





Layer 1



Physical Infrastructure


Dell/HPE Servers · Arista/Mellanox Networking · CtrlS Mumbai/Hyderabad · Sify · NxtGen

title: 5  Introducing Namma Cloud
text:
5.2 What Makes It Different



🇮🇳


5.2.1 Sovereign by Architecture
Every component — hardware, software, networking, data — is designed to stay within Indian borders. No foreign dependencies in the critical path.




🧠


5.2.2 AI-Accelerated Engineering
We leverage AI to compress years of infrastructure development into months. Namma DSL, automated provisioning, ML-based anomaly detection — all AI-enabled.




🔌


5.2.3 Banking-Native Integration
Pre-integrated with NPCI, RBI reporting, UPI networks. No custom integration work needed for Juspay banking products — they run natively on Namma Cloud.




💰


5.2.4 INR Economics
Priced in INR. No FX exposure. Cost structure follows actual compute economics, not hyperscaler margin expansion. Styrofoam catches wastage in real time.

title: 5  Introducing Namma Cloud
text:
5.3 The Multi-DC Architecture
Namma Cloud operates across multiple Tier-4 data centres in India in an active-active configuration. No single point of failure. Instant failover. Zero downtime for region-level events.







🏢 Data Centre Footprint

CtrlS Mumbai · CtrlS Hyderabad · Sify Technologies · NxtGen Datacenter
All Tier-3/4 certified. All within India. All active-active — traffic routes to all regions simultaneously, resources are fully utilised, failover is instant.

title: 6  Core Capabilities
text:
Chapter 5
Five purpose-built components form the heart of Namma Cloud. Each solves a distinct problem that banks face today. Together, they form a complete infrastructure operating system.

title: 6  Core Capabilities
text:
6.1 InfraSwitch — The Operations Brain
InfraSwitch is the central nervous system of Namma Cloud. Every infrastructure change — a deployment, a scaling event, a configuration update, an alert — flows through InfraSwitch's workflow engine.
Built in Haskell with 722 source files, InfraSwitch manages 142+ automated infrastructure workflows across Kubernetes, OpenStack, AWS (for hybrid setups), and GCP. It replaces the 10+ tools most banks use today with a single, unified command centre.


6.1.1 🎛️ Unified Dashboard
Single pane of glass for cluster setup, Kubernetes management, OpenStack operations, scaling, and configuration across all data centres.


6.1.2 🔄 Zero-Downtime Releases
Blue-green deployments with automated A/B testing and staggered rollouts. Every change is monitored, with automatic rollback on anomaly detection.


6.1.3 🔒 Four-Dimensional RBAC
Role × Workflow × Resource Pattern × Cloud Provider — fine-grained access control with full audit trails for every infrastructure change.


6.1.4 🛡️ Crash-Safe by Design
Persistent poll-driven state machine. If a process dies mid-execution, the system resumes from the last checkpoint. At-least-once execution, always.



6.1.5 How InfraSwitch Works
Every infrastructure operation in InfraSwitch is a workflow — a typed, auditable state machine that progresses through pre-check, execute, and validate phases:
-- Every infrastructure change is a ReleaseTracker state machine
CREATED → IN_PROGRESS → UNDER_REVIEW → APPROVED
        → START_RELEASE
        → [Stage 1 .. Stage N execution]
        → STAGGER → MONITORING → POST_MONITORING
        → STABILIZE → COMPLETED

-- On failure: automatic rollback
        → ROLLBACK → REVERT_CHANGES → UNLOCK
Every stage has three phases: PRE_TEST (validate preconditions), EXEC (make the cloud API call), and VALIDATE (poll until confirmed). This three-phase model handles eventual consistency correctly — something most operations tools get wrong.

title: 6  Core Capabilities
text:
6.2 Namma DSL — Infrastructure as Truth
Namma DSL is a Domain Specific Language built in Rust for declaring and managing datacenter infrastructure as code. Its founding philosophy: invalid infrastructure configurations should not compile.
This is not Infrastructure as Code in the Terraform sense. This is Infrastructure as Type-Safe Code — where the compiler enforces correctness before anything touches production. A misconfigured cable type, a rack capacity violation, an invalid CPU configuration — all caught at compile time.



Layer 0



Physical (Metal)


Dell R740/R750, HPE DL385 servers · Arista 7050X3, Mellanox SN2700 switches · 42U racks with power budgets





Layer 1



Operating System / Hosts


Ubuntu / RHEL / Arista / SONiC · Network interface configuration · Zero-touch provisioning via MAAS / Redfish





Layer 2



Services


OpenStack control plane / compute · Ceph storage clusters · Kubernetes nodes · BGP routing (FRR)





6.2.1 The Namma DSL Workflow
-- Step 1: Declare desired state in Rust DSL
spec! {
    Layer0 {
        racks:   [rack_mumbai_1, rack_mumbai_2],
        servers: [dell_r750_01..dell_r750_24],
        switches: [arista_7050x3_top_of_rack],
        links:   [/* typed: Sfp28Cage → DAC cables only */]
    }
    Layer1 {
        hosts:   [/* Ubuntu 22.04 on each server */]
        network: [/* management + data VLANs, separate */]
    }
    Layer2 {
        clusters: [ceph_storage, openstack_control, k8s_prod]
        bgp:      [/* ASNs auto-allocated, neighbors auto-discovered */]
    }
}

-- Step 2: Compile → validates topology invariants
-- Step 3: diff(old_state, desired_state) → deterministic plan
-- Step 4: InfraSwitch executes the plan
-- Step 5: Observe and track reconciliation







✅ Type Safety in Practice

If you try to connect an RJ45Port to an Sfp28Cage, the code won't compile. If you try to fill a 42U rack beyond its power budget, the code won't compile. If you try to deploy a routable network node without a configured host, the code won't compile. Every constraint in your datacenter design is enforced before a single command is run.

title: 6  Core Capabilities
text:
6.3 Autopilot — Zero-Impact Deployments
Autopilot automates the riskiest moment in any software operation: the release. Using blue-green deployments, staggered rollouts, and integrated A/B testing, Autopilot makes every code change a non-event — even for critical payment services.


6.3.1 🟦🟩 Blue-Green Deployments
Two identical environments. New version deployed to green while blue serves live traffic. Instant cutover. Instant rollback.


6.3.2 📊 A/B Statistical Testing
Every release is monitored with binomial, Poisson, and t-test statistical engines. Releases that degrade metrics are automatically aborted before reaching full traffic.


6.3.3 🎯 Staggered Rollouts
5% → 25% → 50% → 100%. Each stage monitored. Automated continue / warn / abort decisions. No human needed in the critical path.

title: 6  Core Capabilities
text:
6.4 Styrofoam — Cost & Capacity Intelligence
Styrofoam is Namma Cloud's predictive capacity planning and cost optimisation engine. It does what every bank wishes its cloud billing dashboard did: tell you in advance what you'll need, and tell you right now what you're wasting.


6.4.1 📈 Predictive Scaling
Uses time-series decomposition (trend + seasonality + residual) to forecast transaction patterns. Scales infrastructure before the load arrives, not after.


6.4.2 🗑️ Wastage Detection
Runs daily. Compares provisioned vs actual usage across EC2, RDS, ElastiCache, and bare metal. Flags over-provisioning with actionable right-sizing recommendations.


6.4.3 💳 Cost Calculator
Multi-cloud pricing engine supporting EC2, EBS, RDS, ElastiCache, Load Balancers, NAT Gateways. Compare AWS vs GCP vs Namma Cloud per workload.


6.4.4 🎯 ML Recommendations
Statistical analysis of p50/p95/p99 metrics with confidence scores. Detects growth trends. Recommends optimal resource sizes with safety headroom.

title: 6  Core Capabilities
text:
6.5 Namma Observe — Full-Stack Visibility
Every layer of Namma Cloud — from bare metal CPU utilisation to UPI transaction success rates — is observable through a single, unified monitoring platform. Not just dashboards. Intelligent, automated observability.

Infrastructure Metrics — CPU, memory, disk, network for every machine. Real-time and historical.
Application Metrics — Business metrics frameworks for payment success rates, latency, throughput per service.
ML Anomaly Detection (Shifu 2.0) — RandomForest, Prophet, IsolationForest models trained on your traffic patterns. Catches anomalies before they become outages.
Automated RCA — Graph-based root cause analysis with temporal causality. When an alert fires, the dashboard of the most anomalous metrics is automatically generated and posted to your Slack.
Mobile Alerts (Jusping) — Firebase push notifications, critical alert sounds, per-alert mute/acknowledge. Your on-call engineer knows about issues before customers do.
Namma DB Tiering — Database storage tiered across SSD → HDD → Tape, with automated data promotion/demotion based on access patterns.

title: 7  Reliability by Design
text:
Chapter 6
99.999% availability is not an aspiration at Juspay — it is an engineering requirement. Every architectural decision in Namma Cloud is made through the lens of: "What happens when this fails?"

title: 7  Reliability by Design
text:
7.1 The Ten Tenets
Juspay's Reliability Principles are not a marketing document. They are the operating philosophy that governs every infrastructure decision, every deployment, and every incident response.




Abundant Redundancy, Active-Active, Instant Failover

No single point of failure at any layer. Every critical component has a symmetric redundant counterpart, actively processing requests. Failover is instant and automatic.





Critical Path — 100X Strong, Utterly Simple

The critical path (payment processing, UPI routing, authentication) must be impossibly simple and impossibly strong. Complexity is the enemy of reliability.





Reduce Blast Radius

Failures should be contained. Merchant-level sharding, namespace-level isolation, circuit breakers between services — every boundary limits the damage of any single failure.





Auto Anomaly Detection, Auto RCA

Failures should be detected and diagnosed before humans are paged. Shifu and Shifu-2.0 provide millisecond anomaly detection and automated root cause analysis.





Dynamic Model-Based Capacity Allocation

Capacity must be allocated based on traffic models, not historical averages. Styrofoam ensures zero bottlenecks by predicting demand and provisioning ahead of it.





Mandatory Staggering, A/B Testing & Rollback for All Changes

No change — code, config, or infrastructure — goes directly to 100% of traffic. Every change is staggered, monitored, and ready to roll back in seconds.





Testing the Limits — Load, Failure, Chaos Tests in Production

We chaos test in production. Load tests validate scale claims. Failure injection tests validate failover assumptions. We test limits before customers find them.





RCA/CoE Process Done with Rigour

Every incident is a Root Cause Analysis. Every RCA is a Culture of Excellence opportunity. No incident is closed without a systemic fix in the critical path.





Set Ambitious SLAs/SLOs — Constantly Improve

99.999% is not a target we declare on slides. It is an SLO we measure in dashboards, review in post-mortems, and improve every quarter.





Org-Wide Collective Ownership

Reliability is not the SRE team's problem. It is every engineer's problem, every product manager's problem, every CXO's problem. The culture reinforces it daily.

title: 7  Reliability by Design
section: 7.2 The Yin and Yang of Reliability
text:
7.2 The Yin and Yang of Reliability
Juspay's reliability philosophy recognises a fundamental tension in distributed systems: the same properties that make a system flexible and scalable (decentralisation, diversity, complexity) are the properties that make it fragile. Reliability requires holding both sides simultaneously.


7.2.1 ⚫ Critical Path (Yin)
Unified · Centralised · Simple · Stable · Certain · Very Strong. The critical path — failover switches, load balancers, rate limiters, authentication — must be narrow, strong, and never fail.


7.2.2 ⚪ Abundance Side (Yang)
Diversity · Redundancy · Distribution · Decentralisation · Complexity · Possibility. The system's resilience comes from abundant redundancy outside the critical path.









💡 The Golden Rule

Every RCA must ask: "Why did the critical path not catch this?" If the CP caught it, it was handled. If it didn't, the CP needs to be strengthened. This recursive improvement loop is why Juspay's uptime keeps improving.





99.999% Target Uptime SLA


<100ms Transaction Latency


50,000+ TPS Tested Capacity


0% Technical Declines

title: 8  Compliance & Sovereignty
text:
Chapter 7
Namma Cloud is the only full-stack cloud infrastructure that is natively built for Indian banking compliance — not retrofitted, not partially compliant, not "working with regulators." Compliant by architecture.

title: 8  Compliance & Sovereignty
text:
8.1 Every Compliance Requirement, Addressed



Requirement
Hyperscaler Status
Namma Cloud




RBI Data Localisation (2018 Circular)
Non-compliant for UPI/payment data
All data on Indian soil, always


MeitY Category-A Data
Explicitly prohibited
Sovereign-certified, Category-A ready

Requires expensive third-party leased lines
Direct NPCI peering, low latency


DPDP Act Compliance
Partial — cross-border transfer risks
Full data residency, consent frameworks


RBI Outsourcing Master Directions
Audit rights, exit clauses complex
Full audit access, Indian jurisdiction


Secret Management
Credentials often on disk or in env vars
OpenBao + IMDS — zero credentials on disk

title: 8  Compliance & Sovereignty
text:
8.2 Secret Management: OpenBao + IMDS
One of the most common — and most dangerous — security anti-patterns in cloud infrastructure is storing credentials on disk or in environment variables. Every bank that has had a credential leak knows this cost.
Namma Cloud uses OpenBao (an open-source, auditable fork of HashiCorp Vault) combined with IMDS (Instance Metadata Service) for secure VM secret access. No credentials ever touch disk. Secrets are fetched at runtime via instance identity, with full audit trails.







🔐 How It Works

A VM that needs a database password does not have the password in its config file. Instead, it presents its IMDS-provided identity token to OpenBao, which validates the identity and returns the secret — temporarily, with an expiry. The secret is never stored. The identity is unforgeable. The audit trail is complete.

title: 8  Compliance & Sovereignty
text:
8.3 Zero Touch Provisioning
New servers in Namma Cloud go from physical delivery to production-ready without a single manual step. The discover → commission → deploy lifecycle is fully automated:
1. Server arrives at data centre
2. Network boots via MAAS/Redfish (PXE)
3. Namma DSL detects new hardware via LLDP discovery
4. OS image deployed automatically (Ubuntu 22.04 LTS)
5. Network interfaces configured from DSL specification
6. BGP neighbours auto-discovered from topology
7. Kubernetes node joins cluster with correct labels/taints
8. OpenBao issues identity certificate
9. Server is production-ready — zero human SSH sessions

title: 8  Compliance & Sovereignty
text:
8.4 The Air-Gap Option
For banks that require complete network isolation for their most sensitive workloads — core banking, RTGS, NEFT infrastructure — Namma Cloud supports fully air-gapped cluster operation. No mandatory internet dependency. No telemetry leaving the premises. Full operational capability in an isolated environment.

title: 9  Cost Economics
text:
Chapter 8
The business case for Namma Cloud is not just regulatory compliance. It is a dramatically better cost structure — with better performance, better visibility, and no FX risk.

title: 9  Cost Economics
text:
9.1 The Total Cost of Cloud
Most comparisons between on-premise and cloud focus on the wrong costs. The real comparison is Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) — including compute, storage, networking, management overhead, compliance spend, FX exposure, and egress fees.
When you add up all of these, the cost of running critical banking workloads on AWS or GCP is significantly higher than the headline compute prices suggest — and it rises every year.

9.1.1 Illustrative Cost Index (Current Hyperscaler = 100)



AWS / GCP Today Index: 100



Baseline — dollar-denominated, FX-exposed, compliance non-trivial





Namma Cloud — Year 1 Index: ~55



~45% lower TCO from year one





Namma Cloud — Year 3 Index: ~35



~65% lower TCO at steady state




Illustrative cost index. Actual savings depend on workload profile, current infrastructure, and migration scope.

title: 9  Cost Economics
text:
9.2 Where the Savings Come From


9.2.1 💱 No FX Risk
INR-denominated pricing. No exposure to dollar fluctuations. No global tariff-driven cost increases. Your infrastructure budget is predictable.


9.2.2 🗄️ Namma DB Tiering
Databases split across SSD → HDD → Tape based on access frequency. Storage costs drop 60–80% versus managed cloud databases without any performance impact on hot data.


9.2.3 🔍 Styrofoam Savings
Daily wastage detection and ML-based right-sizing recommendations reduce infrastructure spend by 20–40%. Most banks provision for peak and forget.


9.2.4 📦 Included Services
Monitoring, CI/CD, secrets management, networking, load balancing — all included. No per-feature surcharges. No egress fees between zones.

title: 9  Cost Economics
section: 9.3 Namma DB Tiering: The Storage Revolution
text:
9.3 Namma DB Tiering: The Storage Revolution
One of the largest and most overlooked costs in banking infrastructure is database storage. Most banks have vast amounts of historical transaction data that is almost never accessed — but they pay SSD prices to store it, because their database doesn't have a cheaper tier.
Namma DB splits data across three storage tiers based on access patterns:
Hot Tier  (NVMe SSD)  — Last 30 days of transaction data
                        UPI/card real-time processing
                        <1ms read latency

Warm Tier (SATA HDD)  — 30 days to 7 years of data
                        Regulatory reporting, audit queries
                        ~10ms read latency

Cold Tier (Tape/S3)  — 7+ years of archived data
                        Legal hold, SEBI/RBI archive requirements
                        Seconds retrieval, 1/20th the cost







💡 The Economics

A bank storing 100TB of historical transaction data on managed RDS today pays approximately ₹2–3 Cr/month. The same data on Namma DB tiering costs ₹25–40L/month — a 75–85% reduction — with identical compliance and retrieval SLAs.

title: 10  Integrated Banking Products
text:
Chapter 9
Every Juspay banking product runs natively on Namma Cloud. For banks that use Juspay products, this means zero integration work and immediate access to a unified infrastructure and application layer.

title: 10  Integrated Banking Products
text:
10.1 One Platform, Every Banking Product
Juspay has spent over a decade building the individual products that power Indian banking. Namma Cloud is the platform that unifies them — the infrastructure layer beneath every product, pre-configured, pre-certified, and ready to run.




UPI Issuer Switch


LIVE


Full UPI stack — onboarding, P2P/P2M payments, UPI Lite, Autopay, credit on UPI. Cloud-native, 0% technical declines.




UPI Consumer Stack


LIVE


In-app UPI with 90%+ conversion rates. No app-switch flows. Cache-based architecture at 10,000+ TPS.




UPI Acquiring


LIVE


Merchant onboarding, offline/online acquisition, QR codes, analytics, reconciliation across all channels.




White Label Cards PG


LIVE


Acquiring + issuing, ACS, tokenization (150Mn+ tokens), 3DS 2.0, smart routing, dispute management.




IBMB (Netbanking 2.0)


LIVE


Intent + QR flows via NBBL switch. Replaces legacy redirection-based netbanking with secure, low-dropout flows.




FASTag


LIVE


Complete FASTag issuing + acquiring stack for highway toll collections and mobility payments.




JusTrust (Fraud)


LIVE


Real-time fraud intelligence with Smart Registry, money flow tracking, mule account detection, and ML risk scoring.




Payment Orchestration


LIVE


Smart routing, tokenization, payins/payouts, white-label checkout, analytics — across all payment methods and networks.




LMS Credit Line


LIVE


Digital lending platform with AI-native collection suite, credit on UPI issuance and acquiring support.









🔌 Zero Integration Overhead

When your bank runs on Namma Cloud, every Juspay product is pre-wired into the infrastructure. No API integration projects. No network configuration. No security review for each product. One infrastructure onboarding unlocks the entire Juspay product catalogue.

title: 11  The Migration Path
text:
Chapter 10
Moving critical banking infrastructure is one of the highest-stakes projects a bank can undertake. Namma Cloud's migration approach is designed around one principle: zero disruption to production banking operations.

title: 11  The Migration Path
text:
11.1 The Strangler Fig Strategy
The strangler fig tree grows around its host, gradually taking its place — but never disrupting it. Namma Cloud's migration strategy follows the same pattern: grow the new system around the old one, validate at every step, and replace components only when the replacement is proven.
This is not a big-bang migration. It is a systematic, phase-by-phase transition where the bank retains full operational capability throughout.



01



Foundation


Months 1–3

Physical DC setup, Namma DSL baseline declaration, network and storage commissioning. Zero-touch provisioning. OpenBao secret management deployed. InfraSwitch connected to the new environment. No production traffic moved yet.




02



Observability Layer


Months 2–5 (overlapping)

Namma Observe deployed to monitor both the existing infrastructure and the new Namma Cloud environment. Anomaly detection baselines established. Alert consolidation — one Slack channel, one mobile app, one dashboard. Shadow mode: Namma Cloud receives traffic but does not process it yet.




03



Non-Critical Workload Migration


Months 4–8

Non-critical workloads — analytics, batch processing, reporting, internal tools — migrated first. Autopilot blue-green deployments. Styrofoam validates cost projections against actuals. Team builds operational muscle before touching production payments.




04



Payment Stack Migration


Months 8–12

UPI, cards, and netbanking workloads migrated with parallel-run strategy. Namma Cloud receives shadow traffic, results compared in real time. Staggered cutover: 5% → 25% → 50% → 100% with A/B statistical validation at each stage. Rollback available at any point.




05



Full Sovereignty


Months 12–18

Complete exit from commercial hyperscalers. All data on Indian sovereign infrastructure. Single pane of glass for all operations. Namma DB tiering in production. Full Juspay product integration live. Hyperscaler contracts terminated.

title: 11  The Migration Path
text:
11.2 What the Bank Provides
Namma Cloud is a managed offering. The bank's team does not need to become experts in Ceph, OpenStack, or Kubernetes internals. Here is what the bank brings to the partnership:

Executive sponsorship and a dedicated migration programme manager
Access to current infrastructure configurations and runbooks
Network connectivity between existing and new environments during parallel running
Business sign-off on cutover windows for each workload
Regulatory liaison for any approvals required from RBI/NPCI








✅ Juspay's Commitment

Juspay provides the full stack: hardware procurement and commissioning, Namma DSL declaration, network design, OpenStack/Ceph/Kubernetes deployment, migration tooling, parallel-run monitoring, cutover execution, and ongoing operations support. The bank's engineers are observers and approvers — not implementors.

title: 12  The Vision Ahead
text:
Chapter 11
Namma Cloud is not a product release. It is a platform strategy — one that gets stronger with every bank that joins, every product that integrates, and every year that the sovereign cloud ecosystem matures.

title: 12  The Vision Ahead
text:
12.1 The Network Effect
Every bank on Namma Cloud strengthens the platform for every other bank. Shared threat intelligence from JusTrust means fraud patterns detected at one bank protect all banks. Shared NPCI connectivity means better performance for everyone. Shared capacity pools mean better utilisation economics for all.
This is the power of a shared sovereign infrastructure — not a compromise on isolation or security, but a multiplier on collective capability.

title: 12  The Vision Ahead
text:
12.2 The 2026–2028 Roadmap


12.2.1 🤖 AI-Native Operations
Namma Cloud's operational intelligence will be AI-native — predictive scaling, automated RCA, self-healing infrastructure. Engineers manage policy, not processes.


12.2.2 🏦 Namma DB — Production Scale
The full Namma DB vision — tiered storage across SSD/HDD/Tape with tenant isolation, full ACID compliance, and banking-grade durability — at production scale for all participants.


12.2.3 🌐 Sovereign Connectivity Fabric
Direct peering with NPCI, SWIFT, and RBI reporting systems — eliminating third-party connectivity costs and compliance complexity for all participating banks.


12.2.4 📱 Developer Platform
A self-service developer platform for bank engineering teams — deploy applications, manage configurations, monitor performance — without needing infrastructure expertise.

title: 12  The Vision Ahead
text:
12.3 The Invitation
India's banking infrastructure needs to be sovereign, modern, and built for the scale of the world's most populous digital economy. The geopolitical moment is here. The regulatory direction is clear. The technology is ready.
Namma Cloud is not a product any single bank could build alone. It requires the combination of infrastructure engineering depth, banking domain knowledge, NPCI relationships, and national-scale operational experience that Juspay has spent 14 years assembling.

"India deserves its own cloud. Not a cloud that happens to have an Indian data centre. A cloud that was designed, from first principles, for Indian banking — by the team that built Indian payments."
— Juspay Technologies, March 2026

title: 12  The Vision Ahead
text:
12.4 Next Steps
We propose a two-hour technical deep dive with your infrastructure and technology leadership. In that session, we will:

Walk through your current infrastructure topology and identify the highest-value migration targets
Demonstrate InfraSwitch and Namma DSL on a live environment
Build a preliminary TCO model for your specific workload profile
Present a milestone-based migration roadmap with risk-adjusted timelines
Address any RBI/NPCI compliance questions with documented evidence








📬 Get in Touch

Email: contact@juspay.in  |  Web: www.juspay.in  |  Office: Bengaluru, India

To schedule the technical deep dive, reach out to your Juspay account team or write directly to the infrastructure team at infra@juspay.in.










Namma Cloud
Eight repositories. One abstraction. One language. One system.
India's sovereign banking infrastructure — built, proven, and ready.
